Equipment Lifecycle
===================

Machinery, equipment and other physical assets are the foundation of industrial enterprises. Factories, plants and similar organizations depend on many pieces of equipment to support their day-to-day operations. Because of this heavy reliance on physical assets, unplanned downtime, outages and equipment failure can cause enormous setbacks for these organizations, resulting in decreased productivity and profits. It’s understandable that industrial enterprises strive to protect their assets to prevent equipment breakdowns and sunk costs.

Physical assets are a huge investment of time and money, and maximizing them for all they are worth involves proper equipment lifecycle management. Through strategic management of the equipment lifecycle, plants and similar organizations can optimize their assets for long-lasting performance and improved ROI.

* * *

What is the equipment lifecycle?
--------------------------------

“Equipment lifecycle” describes the overall lifecycle of a physical asset such as a piece of machinery or equipment. An asset can be as small as a power drill or as large as a jet plane. Regardless of size, every physical asset plays a critical role in supporting the overall productivity of an organization. Therefore, enterprises that heavily rely on physical assets for their operations understand just how important it is to optimize these assets for success. Part of this optimization process involves maximizing the equipment lifecycle so companies can reap the benefits of their assets for as long as possible.

The equipment lifecycle consists of four phases: planning, procurement/acquisition, operation/maintenance and disposal. Each equipment lifecycle phase is critical in supporting the longevity and performance of an asset.

*   **Planning -**The first equipment lifecycle stage is planning for replacing or acquiring a new piece of equipment. Proper planning involves assessing the organization’s needs and determining the most cost-effective strategy for procuring a new asset.

*   **Procurement/Acquisition -**The second phase of the equipment lifecycle is procurement, which means acquiring or purchasing the asset. Before purchase, companies must budget for the asset and negotiate costs to ensure the purchase is as cost-effective as possible. Once the purchase is finalized, the asset is assembled and added to the company’s inventory.

*   **Operation/Maintenance -**This next stage is, ideally, the longest stage of the equipment lifecycle. “Operation” means using the asset for its intended purpose, and “maintenance” means carefully maintaining an asset to support its performance over time. Strategic asset maintenance often incorporates tools such as [CMMS maintenance](https://www.aspentech.com/en/cp/cmms-maintenance)software and [predictive maintenance tools](https://www.aspentech.com/en/cp/predictive-maintenance-tools)to manage and optimize the maintenance process throughout the equipment lifecycle. Organizations can further maximize the operation/maintenance stage by using an [asset management solution](https://www.aspentech.com/en/cp/asset-management-solution)such as [EAM software](https://www.aspentech.com/en/cp/eam-software)to optimize equipment performance throughout the equipment lifecycle.

*   **Disposal -**The final stage of the equipment lifecycle involves properly disposing of the asset. This stage cannot be overlooked, as many organizations are required to follow certain environmental and safety regulations when discarding equipment. The equipment disposal phase entails safely dismantling and disposing of the asset while adhering to important regulations to protect employees and the surrounding environment from any hazards.

* * *

Equipment lifecycle optimization for enhanced results
-----------------------------------------------------

Thanks to modern technology, equipment lifecycle management has never been easier to harness. With tools such as [enterprise asset management](https://www.aspentech.com/en/cp/enterprise-asset-management)software ([EAM software](https://www.aspentech.com/en/cp/eam-software)), CMMS maintenance software and other intelligent solutions, organizations can take better control of the [enterprise asset management](https://www.aspentech.com/en/cp/enterprise-asset-management)process to maximize equipment performance over time. Here are just a few examples of asset management solutions that help support [asset lifecycle management](https://www.aspentech.com/en/cp/asset-lifecycle-management):

*   **[EAM software](https://www.aspentech.com/en/cp/eam-software) -**[EAM software](https://www.aspentech.com/en/cp/eam-software) supports the [asset lifecycle management](https://www.aspentech.com/en/cp/asset-lifecycle-management)process by overseeing all phases of the equipment lifecycle. This powerful software aims to optimize the utilization and quality of assets throughout their lifecycle to improve uptime and decrease operational costs.

*   **[CMMS software](https://www.aspentech.com/en/cp/cmms-software)-**[CMMS software](https://www.aspentech.com/en/cp/cmms-software) may be used as a standalone product or as a component of [EAM software](https://www.aspentech.com/en/cp/eam-software)to support the operation/maintenance stage of the equipment lifecycle. A CMMS centralizes important asset information to streamline equipment maintenance and improve overall efficiency.

*   **[Predictive maintenance tools](https://www.aspentech.com/en/cp/predictive-maintenance-tools)-**Tools such as sensors and gauges can be used in the operation/maintenance stage to detect warning signs of equipment malfunction. Companies can then analyze and apply this information to schedule preventive maintenance and reduce unplanned downtime.

* * *

Why equipment lifecycle management matters
------------------------------------------

For plants, factories and similar enterprises, the performance of their physical assets is directly correlated to profitability. The longer a physical asset can be used, the greater the return on investment. However, utilization only goes so far without optimization, which is where equipment lifecycle management comes into play.

Strategic [asset lifecycle management](https://www.aspentech.com/en/cp/asset-lifecycle-management)requires analyzing equipment performance and making adjustments accordingly to optimize assets for results. By taking advantage of tools such as [EAM software](https://www.aspentech.com/en/cp/eam-software)and other asset management solutions, companies can take a proactive, hands-on approach to asset management and gain a better understanding of how to effectively support the equipment lifecycle.

* * *

FAQs
----

**What is equipment lifecycle?**

 The term “equipment lifecycle” describes the lifespan or longevity of a physical asset, including equipment and machinery. Equipment lifecycle is an important factor in productivity and throughput because the longer a piece of equipment can be used effectively, the better its return on investment. Therefore, companies who rely on physical assets often prioritize maximizing their equipment lifecycle through strategic [enterprise asset management](https://www.aspentech.com/en/cp/enterprise-asset-management).

**What are the four phases of the equipment lifecycle?**

 The four phases of the equipment lifecycle are planning, procurement/acquisition, operation/maintenance, and disposal.

*   **Planning**entails preparing for making an equipment purchase. This stage involves assessing the organization’s needs and determining the best course of action to suit these needs.

*   ****Procurement**, which is also known as “****acquisition****,” involves acquiring or purchasing the asset. Companies must budget accordingly to ensure that the purchase is as cost-efficient as possible. Once the asset is acquired, organizations install or assemble the asset and officially add it to their inventory.**

*   **Operation and maintenance**is the longest stage of the equipment lifecycle. This phase involves using the asset for the purpose for which it was intended. Proper equipment maintenance helps prolong this equipment lifecycle stage. Proper maintenance is imperative for prolonging and optimizing the asset’s lifespan.

*   **Disposal**entails properly discarding the asset. Many industries are bound by various safety and environmental regulations, and proper asset disposal means abiding by such regulations to avoid penalties and ensure the asset is discarded in a safe manner.

Understanding Equipment Lifecycle Management: 9 Key Stages
==========================================================

 November 29, 2023 

##### Summary • 12 minutes read

In this guide, we’ll explore the best practices, strategies, and tools that can help you implement equipment inventory management effectively and transform how you manage your equipment inventory.

##### Share

*   [![Image 25: X](https://redbeam.com/hubfs/raw_assets/public/redbeam-theme-2025/images/icons/x-icon.png)](https://twitter.com/intent/tweet?text=https://redbeam.com/blog/resources/equipment-lifecycle)
*   [![Image 26: Facebook](https://redbeam.com/hubfs/raw_assets/public/redbeam-theme-2025/images/icons/fb-icon.png)](https://www.facebook.com/sharer/sharer.php?u=https://redbeam.com/blog/resources/equipment-lifecycle)
*   [![Image 27: LinkedIn](https://redbeam.com/hubfs/raw_assets/public/redbeam-theme-2025/images/icons/linkedin-icon.png)](http://www.linkedin.com/shareArticle?mini=true&url=https://redbeam.com/blog/resources/equipment-lifecycle)
*   [![Image 28: Email](https://redbeam.com/hubfs/raw_assets/public/redbeam-theme-2025/images/icons/email-icon.png)](mailto:?subject=Understanding%20Equipment%20Lifecycle%20Management:%209%20Key%20Stages&body=https://redbeam.com/blog/resources/equipment-lifecycle)

![Image 29](https://20597294.fs1.hubspotusercontent-na1.net/hub/20597294/hubfs/1.Featured-3.png?width=1062&name=1.Featured-3.png)

In pursuing innovation, businesses often find themselves trapped in a paradox. They invest in cutting-edge equipment, only to see it become outdated. This paradox raises a crucial question. Can you innovate without mastering the art of equipment lifecycle management?

Despite its importance, equipment lifecycle management remains uncharted territory for many businesses. Neglecting this aspect of operations can be devastating. Think: Soaring costs and an inability to adapt to evolving market demands.

Today, we confront the innovation paradox head-on. Discover how to break free from endless spending. Harness your equipment's true potential. Rework lifecycle management and redirect resources for innovation. This guide can show you how.

**Main takeaways from this article:**

*   Properly managing assets from acquisition to disposal reduces unexpected equipment breakdowns, lowers maintenance costs, and extends equipment lifespan.
*   Regular maintenance and timely upgrades improve equipment performance, ensuring reliable and efficient operations with minimal downtime.
*   Using data and analytics helps businesses make informed decisions about equipment upgrades, replacements, and budgeting, maximizing return on investment.
*   RedBeam’s asset management software provides real-time equipment tracking, real-time monitoring, centralized documentation, and data to help you optimize equipment performance and efficiency.

**What Is the Equipment Lifecycle?**
------------------------------------

The equipment management life cycle encompasses the entire journey of a piece of equipment, from initial acquisition to its eventual retirement. It includes stages such as proper planning, design, procurement, deployment, operation, maintenance, and disposal.

Each stage involves specific processes to ensure optimal performance, safety, and compliance with environmental regulations. Proper and careful planning at each phase maximizes efficiency, extends the equipment's lifespan, and reduces costs.

**Why Equipment Lifecycle Management Matters**
----------------------------------------------

Many businesses find it difficult to save for the future while investing in the present. Choosing cheaper, temporary fixes may save money initially, but it can lead to more expenses later on. These can include higher upkeep costs, frequent replacements, and operational problems. Investing in better equipment is more expensive but can [save money in the long run](https://www.investopedia.com/terms/c/capital-investment.asp#:~:text=By%20investing%20in%20new%20equipment,to%20cost%20savings%20over%20time.). These savings manifest through reduced equipment maintenance expenses, increased productivity, and minimized downtime.

**Equipment Lifecycle Example**
-------------------------------

Here’s an example of how the equipment lifecycle can progress. Let’s take a look at a single item. In this case, a laptop.

*   **Planning and design:** The IT department assesses the need for new laptops, considering factors like performance requirements, budget constraints, and compatibility with existing systems.
*   **Procurement:** Based on the specifications, the IT team researches various laptop models and vendors. They obtain competitive bids, negotiate terms, and select the most suitable laptops.
*   **Deployment:** Once the laptops arrive, the IT team prepares them for use, making sure the appropriate software and security measures are installed.
*   **Operation:** Employees receive their laptops, get any necessary training, and begin using them for daily tasks.
*   **Maintenance:** Throughout the laptop's lifespan, software updates and security patches are applied and performance is monitored to keep an eye on equipment health.
*   **Disposal:** When equipment becomes outdated or performance degrades beyond repair, laptops are securely wiped and disposed of. Any salvageable components are repurposed if possible.

This lifecycle ensures that the company maximizes the value of its IT assets while maintaining security and efficiency throughout its usable life.

**Benefits of Equipment Lifecycle Management**
----------------------------------------------

Benefits of long-term investments in equipment lifecycle management include:

### **Reduced Costs and Downtime**

Effective equipment life cycle management minimizes unexpected breakdowns and costly emergency repairs. By implementing proactive maintenance schedules and doing timely repairs and upgrades, businesses can reduce operational interruptions and extend the lifespan of their equipment.

This approach lowers repair expenses and minimizes unplanned downtime, ensuring consistent productivity and reducing revenue loss.

### **Improved Equipment Performance**

Well-managed equipment performs more efficiently and reliably. Regular maintenance tasks and timely upgrades prevent wear and tear, enhancing operational efficiency and safety. By monitoring performance metrics and addressing potential issues early, you can optimize your equipment's output and maintain high-quality standards, ultimately boosting productivity.

### **Stronger Return on Investment (ROI)**

Investing in proper equipment lifecycle management maximizes the value derived from each piece of equipment. By extending its operational life and maintaining optimal performance, businesses achieve a [stronger ROI](https://redbeam.com/roi). This approach reduces the need for frequent replacements and allows organizations to fully capitalize on their initial investments.

### **Improved Planning and Forecasting**

A comprehensive view of the equipment lifecycle enables better planning and budgeting. By understanding when you need equipment maintenance, upgrades, or replacement, businesses can make informed financial decisions. This foresight supports more accurate forecasting, ensuring resource availability and minimizing unexpected expenses.

### **Enhanced Compliance and Safety**

Effective lifecycle management helps businesses comply with industry standards and safety regulations. By maintaining equipment according to regulatory requirements, organizations minimize safety risks and avoid costly fines or legal complications. Ensuring safety also protects employees and promotes a healthier work environment.

### **Sustainability and Environmental Responsibility**

Properly managing equipment throughout its lifecycle supports sustainability efforts by minimizing waste and maximizing resource use. Proper disposal and recycling of outdated equipment reduce environmental impact. Additionally, energy-efficient upgrades can decrease overall energy consumption, contributing to a more sustainable operation.

**Key Stages of the Equipment Lifecycle**
-----------------------------------------

Effective equipment lifecycle management involves a series of stages, each playing a critical role in maximizing efficiency, reducing equipment costs, and ensuring the safe and sustainable use of equipment. Understanding these stages helps organizations make informed decisions, optimize operations, and strategically plan for future needs.

Let's explore each phase in detail:

### **1. Planning and Design**

Key steps:

*   Research and conceptualization of the equipment's specifications and requirements
*   Detailed planning involving engineers, designers, and stakeholders

During the planning and design phase, organizations identify their operational needs and outline the specifications required for new equipment. This stage involves extensive research, feasibility studies, and collaboration with engineers, designers, and stakeholders to ensure the equipment meets safety, efficiency, and regulatory standards.

By anticipating potential challenges and designing solutions accordingly, businesses can prevent costly redesigns or modifications later in the asset’s lifecycle.

### **2. Procurement and Acquisition**

Key steps:

*   Sourcing and purchasing the equipment from reliable suppliers
*   Negotiating contracts, ensuring quality standards, and budget adherence

Equipment purchases focus on selecting and acquiring the right equipment to meet organizational needs. It includes evaluating suppliers, negotiating contracts, and ensuring the equipment meets quality and budget requirements. As items are procured, they are entered into the asset management software systems and [asset tags](https://redbeam.com/features/equipment-tagging-software) are applied.

During this phase, you will also consider what data you want to include in your asset tags and asset tracking system. For example, for [IT hardware asset management](https://redbeam.com/features/it-asset-management-software), you may want to include equipment maintenance logging and condition assessment.

Proper procurement practices prevent delays, reduce costs, and ensure compliance with industry standards. Effective supplier relationships also pave the way for long-term support and proper equipment maintenance.

### **3. Installation and Commissioning**

Key steps:

*   Physical installation of the equipment at the designated location
*   Testing and calibrating to ensure proper functionality and safety

Once acquired, the equipment is installed at the designated site. This process involves setting up the equipment, integrating it with existing systems, and performing tests to verify its functionality, safety, and efficiency.

Proper commissioning ensures that the equipment operates as intended and meets all regulatory requirements. Any issues detected during this phase can be resolved before full-scale operation, minimizing potential disruptions.

### **4. Operation and Maintenance**

Key steps:

*   Regular operation of the equipment in alignment with manufacturer guidelines
*   Routine preventative maintenance to prevent breakdowns and ensure longevity

During this stage, the equipment is actively used in daily business operations. Routine maintenance of your tracked equipment, including inspections, cleaning, lubrication, and part replacements, is conducted to prevent malfunctions and extend the equipment’s lifespan.

Proper operation and proper maintenance practices enhance safety, efficiency, and productivity while minimizing unexpected downtime and costly repairs.

### **5. Upgrades and Modifications**

Key steps:

*   Implementing upgrades to enhance efficiency or adhere to new standards
*   Modifications to adapt the equipment to changing needs or technological advancements

To keep up with evolving technological standards and operational requirements, equipment may require upgrades or modifications. This phase involves enhancing existing systems to improve performance, safety, and efficiency.

Upgrades can include software updates, hardware enhancements, or adapting equipment to new regulations. Proactive upgrades help organizations maintain a competitive edge and extend the equipment's lifecycle.

### **6. Decommissioning and Disposal**

Key steps:

*   Shutting down the equipment at the end of its useful life
*   Disassembling, recycling, or disposing of components according to regulations

When equipment reaches the end of its useful life, it is decommissioned and safely removed from service. This stage includes shutting down operations, disassembling the equipment, and disposing of your valuable equipment or recycling components according to environmental regulations.

Proper decommissioning minimizes the environmental impact, ensures safety, and complies with legal standards.

### **7. Replacement**

Key steps:

*   Identifying suitable replacements for obsolete or inefficient equipment
*   Transitioning to new equipment to avoid disruptions

Replacement involves evaluating outdated or inefficient equipment and selecting suitable alternatives. This stage ensures a seamless transition to new equipment with minimal operational disruption.

Strategic replacement planning helps organizations maintain productivity while leveraging the latest technological advancements for improved efficiency and cost savings.

### **8. Asset Retirement**

Key steps:

*   Documenting the retirement process, including disposal methods and environmental impact
*   Archiving relevant data for future reference or compliance purposes

Asset retirement involves formally documenting the disposal of the retired equipment and accounting for any residual value in your accounting process. This step also includes archiving maintenance records, decommissioning procedures, and compliance reports.

Proper documentation ensures regulatory compliance and provides valuable insights for future procurement and lifecycle management decisions.

### **9. Monitoring and Optimization**

Key steps:

*   Continuous monitoring and continuous improvement of equipment performance and energy usage
*   Implementing optimizations based on data analysis to improve operations and cost-effectiveness.

Throughout the equipment lifecycle, continuous monitoring and data analysis of critical operations are key to maintaining optimal performance. By tracking key performance indicators (KPIs), energy consumption, and maintenance needs, organizations can proactively identify inefficiencies and implement cost-effective optimizations.

This stage supports data-driven decision-making and enhances the overall efficiency and sustainability of equipment operations.

# The 4 stages of asset lifecycle management - what you need to know

![](https://www.expansivefm.com/hubfs/tom-author-profile.png)
![The 4 stages of asset lifecycle management - what you need to know](https://www.expansivefm.com/hubfs/assset_lifecycle_3.jpeg)

There are four stages to the classic asset lifecycle: planning, acquisition, maintenance and disposal. But what data and digital tools will take the guesswork and potential disruption out of the way we manage each stage?

What’s needed to extend the operational life of your assets and ensure their seamless replacement when the time comes?

## Stage 1: Planning

Knowing the right time to acquire new assets and [replace existing equipment](/latest/cafm-solution-asset-lifecycle-management) will depend on the data available to you. If you’ve been tracking and recording their service history within dedicated management software, you should have the tools to see when equipment is failing more frequently and likely to need renewing.

But if you haven’t, it might simply be a final and catastrophic failure that signals you need to invest again. In the world of purely reactive maintenance and unmanaged assets, the danger of unplanned expenses ruining your [budget planning](/category/latest-news/maximise-maintenance-budgets-with-fm-software) always looms large.

In an ideal world, decision-makers will identify the need for asset acquisition, then base their procurement decisions around data, including the value an asset is projected to bring to the business and its potential lifespan. The need for performance and longevity will be balanced against the budget available for the purchase.

If you’ve been tracking the acquisition and maintenance costs of the asset throughout its life, this data should inform that calculation and give you a good idea about how much you could and should be spending on a replacement.

![4 stages of asset lifecycle management](https://www.expansivefm.com/hs-fs/hubfs/lifecycle-of-asset-management.webp?width=600&height=570&name=lifecycle-of-asset-management.webp)

## Stage 2: Acquisition

When the asset is acquired and deployed it should be added to an asset register. In some businesses this will be no more than an [Excel sheet,](/category/latest-news/excel-vs-cafm) while others will use a digital asset register to record full details about the equipment.

Whatever the choice you make, the more detail you capture about the asset in your records, the more information you will have to inform your decision making throughout its lifecycle. These details should include:

When these details are captured in a digital asset register operating as part of a CAFM this data can use be used to:

Estimate asset lifecycle for budget/replacement planning

Most modern-day management systems will also include asset tracking solutions such as RFID and QR labelling. When an asset is acquired a digital tag is attached to it, linking the equipment to the records in the register itself including its full service history.

## Stage 3: Utilisation and maintenance

In this stage of the asset lifecycle the FM team use reactive, planned and preventative maintenance to improve operational efficiency:

Depending on the sophistication of the digital tools you’re using, there will be a balance of [reactive](/category/latest-news/iceberg-the-unseen-costs-of-reactive-maintenance-vs-ppm) and [planned maintenance](/category/latest-news/preventative-maintenance) designed to keep the asset working and compliant - while extending its useful life for as long as possible.

Using maintenance management software such as a CAFM will ensure a business is carrying out the right work on the right equipment at the right time.  It gives you the tools to manage and track asset performance and asset health. Workflow automation ensures:

Throughout this stage of the equipment lifecycle, good CAFM software should ensure engineers and contractors are:

In this way, the CAFM builds the data around asset usage and condition - as well as the costs involved in keeping them running.

Over time, the business will be able to see patterns in asset performance and condition that might give cause for concern (multiple breakdowns, increased frequency of breakdowns). And they will be able to see when the cost of maintaining the asset is likely to overshadow the cost of replacing it altogether.

## Stage 4: Disposal

At the end of its working life, the asset needs to be disposed of,  re-purposed, sold off or recycled. With a greater  ESG focus on the [circular economy](https://ellenmacarthurfoundation.org/topics/circular-economy-introduction/overview) and increasing [costs associated with asset disposal,](https://www.ie-uk.com/blog/office-furniture-environmenal-impact) it's becoming more and more important you can effectively monitor and control 'end-of-life'.

If you’ve only managed the asset on a reactive basis across its lifespan and have little or no data available about its performance it will always be more difficult to accurately predict when you will need to replace the equipment.  The business risks replacing the asset too soon (before it’s strictly necessary) or making an emergency replacement when there’s a complete failure. In both cases, money, time and resource could be wasted.

On the other hand, businesses who have been maintaining and tracking the asset throughout its lifecycle using a digital asset register powered by a CAFM system will likely have all the right data available to ensure the best timing of a replacement.

Data acquired throughout the asset lifecycle available within a CAFM will help the business plan budgets more effectively. The use of dashboards, graphs and tables in FM software will help businesses visualise the deterioration of specific asset types, potential ongoing costs for supporting failing assets and help the business realise and plan for major Capex well in advance of the need.

At the same time, all the data around lifecycle, usage, performance, maintenance costs and ROI you have acquired can be utilised in the planning phase (see above) of the next acquisition cycle. It can help you make sure you buy the right equipment to meet your business needs.

## Conclusion

Asset lifecycle management software is integral to building the  processes you need in place to gain ISO 50000 (the global asset management standard).  But every business can benefit from the control over asset lifespan and condition that a good CAFM system brings with it.

Lifecycle management can have a dramatic influence on the effectiveness and overall profitability of a business.

If equipment is prone to failure and is badly maintained it can cause business downtime, drive customers away and drain revenue.

Without the right software, asset maintenance can be reactive, siloed and short-sighted in the way it services assets and manages budget. If critical assets are not maintained effectively, you may be risking H&S, regulatory breaches and subsequent fines.

Asset management done right, however, can increase business revenue, customer satisfaction and get the entire business working together to deliver on shared objectives.

As the IAM states in this [video primer](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZVo-56Crex0&ab_channel=TheIAM), good asset management strategy ‘maximises the long term value of the assets by aligning them to the objectives and purpose of an organisation’.

The right asset management software will help you;

The right CAFM is a tool for everyone in a company to understand and manage the operational and financial effect the asset lifecycle has on a business, ensuring that [strategy is completely aligned](/strategic-asset-management).

The 4 Key Stages of the Asset Lifecycle
=======================================

 October 27, 2023 

If you want to optimize asset performance and ensure efficient operations, you absolutely need to understand the asset lifecycle. Every asset – from the boiler in the basement to the shingles on the roof – has an optimum operating period of peak performance. After a certain period of use, wear and tear takes its toll as the asset ages. Eventually, every asset must be replaced.

Knowing where each asset is in its lifecycle helps you avoid unplanned downtime and stay ahead of replacements. Making smart decisions at every stage of the asset lifecycle helps you improve asset efficiency and saves your organization money over the long run. So, without further ado, here are the **four key stages of the asset lifecycle**and how you can use them to your advantage.

The 4 Asset Lifecycle Stages: Explained
---------------------------------------

### Stage #1: Planning

The planning stage determines the [current condition of equipment and other building assets](https://home.akitabox.com/blog/facility-condition-assessment) and the most cost-effective means of moving forward. A building manager may:

*   **Assess**whether or not current equipment meets production needs
*   **Ensure** that necessary resources are available for use
*   **Identify** which assets are underperforming or unnecessary to operations
*   **Check** that equipment is receiving proper levels of maintenance
*   **Estimate**[funding required](https://home.akitabox.com/blog/5-ways-improve-capital-planning/) to procure a replacement asset

If a machine or piece of equipment is underperforming, the next step is to research available replacement options, make comparisons, and conduct demos or trials (if applicable).

**Read More: [The Essential Guide to Facility Asset Management](https://home.akitabox.com/blog/facility-asset-management/)**

![Image 18](https://home.akitabox.com/wp-content/uploads/2020/02/Blog-The-4-Key-Stages-of-the-Equipment-Lifecycle-Subimage-1.webp)

### Stage #2: Purchase and Acquisition

This stage is about [defining the costs and requirements](https://home.akitabox.com/blog/reduce-building-maintenance-costs) of purchasing the asset – with the goal of making the acquisition as cost-effective as possible.

It’s important to **t**ake into account the asset’s initial investment costs as well as the its [total cost of ownership](https://home.akitabox.com/blog/calculate-equipment-depreciation-life). Finally, the asset is installed and added to the organization’s [asset management software](https://home.akitabox.com/software/akitabox-platform/#asset-management).

### Stage #3: Operation and Maintenance

This is typically the longest and most substantial stage of an asset’s useful life. In this stage, **operation and maintenance activities are performed and tracked**, typically in a [software platform](https://home.akitabox.com/software/akitabox-platform/#maintenance-management). Building management teams focus on keeping the asset in good working condition to ensure efficiency and productivity, using [preventive maintenance](https://home.akitabox.com/blog/preventive-maintenance-101) programs, regular [inspection schedules](https://home.akitabox.com/blog/inspection-reporting-building-audits), specialized [cleaning routines,](https://home.akitabox.com/blog/create-a-custodial-cleaning-plan) and regular routine maintenance.

The operation and maintenance stage can be broken down into 3 Rs: **Repair, Rehabilitate,** and **Replace.** In other words, assets should be monitored to ensure that repairs, parts replacements, and adjustments are done in a timely manner to ensure ideal functionality.

### Stage #4: Renew or Dispose

As long as an asset is functioning correctly, it’s within its useful life. But there’ll come a time when the asset’s performance significantly deteriorates. Assets that are approaching (or exceeding) their life expectancy will experience age-related wear and tear or outright failure. Equipment that fails to meet production quotas or efficiency standards ultimately impacts an entire organization’s success. In this situation, it’s time to renew or dispose of the asset.

In the wake of asset failure, [asset management software](https://home.akitabox.com/product/platform/location-based-asset-mapping) can provide the asset lifecycle data needed to inform the best way forward. A good rule of thumb is that i**f the cost to repair an asset is more than the cost to replace it, it’s most cost-efficient to dispose** of (aka “decommission”) the asset. Once an asset is decommissioned, that asset’s lifecycle is over and a new asset’s life begins again at stage 1.

Why Does Asset Lifecycle Management Matter?
-------------------------------------------

Properly managing the lifecycles of your equipment has a significant impact on your organization’s efficiency, cost control, and overall performance.

*   **Clearly defines your assets and their conditions.** Good asset management software lets you record the installation date, condition, and cost-effectiveness of each piece of equipment. This data can be used to forecast an asset’s estimated useful life and total cost of ownership.
*   **Supports a proactive approach to maintenance.** Asset lifecycle management helps a business actively manage the physical health of its machinery. [Proactive maintenance](https://home.akitabox.com/blog/what-is-preventive-maintenance) enables organizations to better predict future scenarios, plan for asset failure, and take well-planned steps regarding maintenance and eventual replacement.

![Image 19](https://home.akitabox.com/wp-content/uploads/2020/02/Blog-The-4-Key-Stages-of-the-Equipment-Lifecycle-Subimage-2.webp)

*   **Supports regulatory compliance in your organization**. With the help of facilities management software, your team can collect and use valuable lifecycle data for each critical asset. This way, your team can catch existing problems before they become a compliance issue with regulatory organizations (like OSHA) or accrediting agencies (like the Joint Commission).
*   **Helps prioritize equipment and machinery replacements.**Creating and maintaining a [capital budget](https://home.akitabox.com/blog/communicate-facility-management-team-value) is an important part of managing a building. By staying up-to-date on asset conditions and life expectancies, your team can more accurately [forecast funding](https://home.akitabox.com/product/app/real-property-asset-management) needs and ensure maintenance reserves are sufficient.

In this stage, organizations acquire the necessary equipment through budgeting, vendor negotiations, and purchasing strategies, ensuring alignment with operational goals and budget constraints.

This critical phase focuses on utilizing equipment effectively while performing regular maintenance activities, using tools like CMMS and predictive maintenance to optimize performance and minimize downtime.
The final stage entails safely decommissioning and disposing of equipment, adhering to environmental regulations and ensuring proper recycling or disposal to mitigate environmental impact.
